card.intuition: Assign every point to its nearest centroid, then move each centroid to the average of its assigned points — repeat until nothing moves.
card.analogy: Dividing a city into k fire station zones: each station claims the nearest houses, then relocates to the center of its zone, then re-claims — repeat until the zones stabilize.
card.time_complexity: O(n × k × i × d) — n points, k clusters, i iterations, d dimensions
when_to_use: You know (or can estimate) the number of clusters k
Clusters are roughly spherical and similarly sized
Fast exploratory clustering on large datasets
gotchas: Sensitive to initialization — bad starting centroids cause poor convergence; use k-means++ to mitigate
k must be chosen in advance — use the elbow method or silhouette score to pick k
Fails on non-convex or unequal-density clusters — use DBSCAN or GMM instead

exercise.prompt: Complete the assignment step of k-means: assign each point to the nearest centroid.
exercise.starter_code:
import numpy as np

def assign_clusters(X, centroids):
    """
    X: (n, d) array of points
    centroids: (k, d) array of centroid positions
    Returns: (n,) array of cluster indices
    """
    distances = np.linalg.norm(
        X[:, np.newaxis, :] - ___________,
        axis=2
    )  # shape (n, k)
    return ___________
exercise.solution:
centroids[np.newaxis, :, :]  # broadcast centroids across all points
np.argmin(distances, axis=1)  # index of nearest centroid for each point
hints: You need the distance from every point to every centroid — broadcasting X (n,1,d) against centroids (1,k,d) gives a (n,k,d) tensor
After computing the (n,k) distance matrix, argmin along axis=1 gives the nearest centroid index for each point
exercise.test_code:
import numpy as np
X = np.array([[1.,0.],[0.,1.],[10.,0.],[10.,1.]])
c = np.array([[0.,0.],[10.,0.]])
L = assign_clusters(X,c)
assert L.shape == (4,), f'Shape must be (4,): {L.shape}'
assert L[0]==0, f'[1,0]->c0, got {L[0]}'
assert L[1]==0, f'[0,1]->c0, got {L[1]}'
assert L[2]==1, f'[10,0]->c1, got {L[2]}'
assert L[3]==1, f'[10,1]->c1, got {L[3]}'
print('PASS')
